sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is frequently diagnosed in the setting of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis, which itself may be asymptomatic. When symptoms occur, they are often nonspecific and related to advanced disease or hepatic decompensation. Classic presentation includes an elevated serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) level discovered during surveillance of a high-risk patient, or the identification of a liver nodule on imaging performed for other reasons. Patients may present with right upper quadrant pain, a palpable mass, weight loss, anorexia, malaise, or jaundice. Physical signs can include hepatomegaly, ascites, or features of chronic liver disease such as spider angiomata or palmar erythema. Paraneoplastic syndromes, although rare, can occur and include hypercholesterolemia, erythrocytosis, hypercalcemia, and hypoglycemia. The diagnosis of combined hepatocellular-cholangiocarcinoma (cHCC-CCA) is challenging, as it may present similarly to HCC but can also be associated with elevated CA 19-9.

symptoms.name: Right upper quadrant pain
symptoms.detail: Nonspecific pain or discomfort in the area of the liver.

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Yellowing of the skin and sclera, indicating impaired bilirubin metabolism.
symptoms.frequency: Less common at presentation; more typical of advanced disease

symptoms.name: Ascites
symptoms.detail: Accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity.
symptoms.frequency: Seen in patients with advanced cirrhosis or peritoneal involvement

symptoms.name: Palpable mass
symptoms.detail: A mass felt in the right upper quadrant.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; indicates larger tumor size
